content.text: Organizing Python code into modules and packages is one of the most impactful structural decisions in any agent project. A well-designed package layout separates tools from LLM clients, keeps orchestration logic independent of external integrations, and makes every component independently testable — qualities that become critical as agent codebases grow beyond a handful of files.
file. When Python imports a module, every top-level name in that file — functions, classes, constants — becomes an attribute of the resulting module object. Modules can be imported with
, or specific names brought into scope with
is a directory that contains an
file (or, since Python 3.3, a namespace package with no
at all). Packages let you group related modules under a common namespace. Subpackages add another directory level. When Python encounters

The distinction matters for agentic AI projects because a typical agent system has at least four distinct concerns: the LLM client (which calls the language model), tool implementations (which execute actions), orchestration logic (which decides what to do next), and configuration (API keys, model settings, retry limits). Each concern maps naturally to one or more modules inside a well-named package.

Agent projects start small — often a single script that calls an API and prints a result. Within days, that script typically gains tool functions, retry logic, prompt templates, and a CLI. Without intentional structure, the file becomes impossible to test in isolation, difficult to understand, and fragile when any single piece changes.
Modular structure solves this by enforcing boundaries. When

, it depends on the public interface of the tools package, not on how search works internally. Changing the search implementation never touches the runner. Adding a new tool never touches existing tools. Each module can be tested independently with a mock or stub for its dependencies.

There is also a practical deployment concern. Agent services commonly expose the same logic through a FastAPI endpoint, a CLI, and a test harness. A package-based layout lets all three entry points import the same modules without duplication. Shared imports mean a bug fix in one place fixes it everywhere.
— LLM client, tools, orchestration, and config live in distinct modules with clear boundaries
— the same tool functions are imported by both the agent loop and unit tests without duplication
re-exports only the names callers should use; implementation details stay private
— isolated modules can be tested with lightweight mocks; no need to instantiate the full agent graph
— module-level code (database connections, model loads) runs the first time the module is imported, which slows test startup and can cause failures if the environment is not set up
— when module A imports B and B imports A, Python raises an
or returns a partially-initialized module; this is the most common structural bug in Python packages

differently than running it as a module (
), breaking relative imports in the first form
— splitting a 20-line utility into five one-function modules adds cognitive overhead without benefit; use judgement about granularity
imports every public name from tools, potentially shadowing built-ins or creating hard-to-debug name collisions
, it performs these steps in order:
— if the module is already cached, return the cached object immediately (Python imports each module only once per interpreter session)
Create a module object and add it to
Execute the module body top-to-bottom in the module's own namespace
Return the module object to the caller

The caching in step 1 is what prevents infinite loops when two modules import each other — but it also means that if a circular import occurs before one module has finished executing, the importing module may see an incomplete object.
Inside a package, modules can use either absolute or relative imports. Absolute imports reference the full path from the top of the package:
. Relative imports use dot notation to navigate the package tree:
(parent package). Both forms work; most style guides recommend absolute imports for clarity, reserving relative imports for tight intra-package references where the full path is verbose.
file runs when the package is first imported. Its primary purposes are: re-exporting selected names so callers can write
exports; and running any package-level initialization that must happen before any module in the package is used.

# tools/__init__.py — clean facade pattern
from .search import search
from .calculator import calculate
from .code_exec import run_code

__all__ = ["search", "calculate", "run_code"]
# Callers now write: from tools import search
# Internal module names (search.py) are an implementation detail

, Python treats it as a namespace package. This is useful for splitting a large package across multiple directories or repositories (plugin systems), but it comes with a cost: namespace packages do not run initialization code on import because there is no
to run. For agent projects, regular packages with explicit
files are almost always the right choice.

The diagram below shows a typical agentic AI package layout with four sub-packages and the import flow between them. The arrow direction indicates the dependency direction: agents import from clients and tools; both clients and tools import from config; nothing imports from agents except the entry point.

attrs.alt: Agent package architecture showing import flow between config, clients, tools, and agents sub-packages

content.text: This layout enforces a strict dependency DAG (directed acyclic graph): entry point → agents → clients/tools → config. No module imports from a level above it in the hierarchy, which eliminates circular imports by construction.
Break a single-file agent into a package as soon as any of these conditions apply:
— each tool family deserves its own module (
— if both a CLI and a FastAPI server import agent logic, that logic should live in an importable package, not a script
— retry logic, token counting, or prompt templates used in more than one place belong in a module, not duplicated
— the moment unit tests need to import agent code, a package structure with

Conversely, a single script is appropriate for a throwaway prototype, a one-off data extraction job, or a demonstration that will never be extended. The cost of package structure is upfront; the benefit compounds with project size.
to split into sub-packages: resist creating sub-packages for single modules. A
sub-package makes sense when there are three or more tool modules. A
, adds directory overhead for no organizational benefit — just use

my_agent/
  __init__.py         # package root; keep nearly empty
  config.py           # settings, env var loading
  clients/
    __init__.py       # re-exports LLMClient
    llm.py            # Anthropic / OpenAI client wrapper
  tools/
    __init__.py       # re-exports all tool functions
    search.py         # web search tool
    calculator.py     # arithmetic tool
    code_exec.py      # sandboxed code execution
  agents/
    __init__.py
    runner.py         # main agent loop
tests/
  tools/
    test_search.py
    test_calculator.py
  agents/
    test_runner.py
pyproject.toml        # project metadata + entry points

[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools>=68"]
build-backend = "setuptools.backends.legacy:build"

[project]
name = "my-agent"
version = "0.1.0"
requires-python = ">=3.11"
dependencies = [
    "anthropic>=0.25",
    "httpx>=0.27",
]

[project.scripts]
agent = "my_agent.__main__:main"

[tool.setuptools.packages.find]
where = ["."]
once. The package is now importable from anywhere in the project and from test runners — no manual
Step 3 — Implement config.py with lazy loading

# my_agent/config.py
"""Central configuration — reads environment variables once on import."""
import os
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Config:
    anthropic_api_key: str
    model: str
    max_tokens: int
    max_retries: int

def load_config() -> Config:
    key = os.environ.get("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY", "")
    if not key:
        raise RuntimeError("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is not set")
    return Config(
        anthropic_api_key=key,
        model=os.environ.get("AGENT_MODEL", "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022"),
        max_tokens=int(os.environ.get("AGENT_MAX_TOKENS", "4096")),
        max_retries=int(os.environ.get("AGENT_MAX_RETRIES", "3")),
    )

# Module-level instance — safe because Config only reads env vars
# Call load_config() at function scope if the test suite needs to override env vars
_config: Config | None = None

def get_config() -> Config:
    global _config
    if _config is None:
        _config = load_config()
    return _config

# my_agent/__main__.py
"""Allows: python -m my_agent [task]"""
import sys
from my_agent.agents.runner import run

def main() -> None:
    task = " ".join(sys.argv[1:]) or "Hello, agent!"
    result = run(task)
    print(result)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
This self-contained example demonstrates a two-module package that can be run immediately. Create both files, then run

# echo_agent/__init__.py
# (empty — just marks the directory as a package)

# echo_agent/tools.py
def echo(text: str) -> str:
    """Return the input unchanged — a trivial tool for demonstration."""
    return f"[echo] {text}"

# echo_agent/__main__.py
from echo_agent.tools import echo
import sys

if __name__ == "__main__":
    user_input = " ".join(sys.argv[1:]) or "hello"
    print(echo(user_input))

Example 2 — Tool registry with __all__ and lazy import
Consider a production agent service that exposes tools through a FastAPI endpoint, processes tasks in a CLI, and is tested with pytest. All three entry points share the same package:

— a private attribute in the client module — without touching the runner, the tools, or config. This is possible only because the modules are separate and have clear boundaries. In a monolithic script, patching anything requires rewriting the entire file.

At scale, the same package is published to a private PyPI registry and installed as a dependency by the API server, the CLI container, and the CI pipeline. Version pinning ensures every environment uses the same code.
The most common structural bug. It happens when two modules each import the other:

# WRONG — circular dependency
# agents/runner.py
from tools.search import search      # tools/search.py imports runner for logging

# tools/search.py
from agents.runner import log_call   # ImportError: cannot import partially-initialized module

# FIX 1 — restructure: extract shared code into a third module
# utils/logging.py
def log_call(name: str, args: dict) -> None:
    print(f"[tool] {name}({args})")

# tools/search.py — now imports from utils, not agents
from utils.logging import log_call

# FIX 2 — lazy import inside the function (avoids the cycle without restructuring)
def search(query: str) -> list[dict]:
    from agents.runner import log_call   # imported at call time, not module load time
    log_call("search", {"query": query})
    ...

# WRONG — connecting to a database at import time
import psycopg2
conn = psycopg2.connect("postgresql://localhost/agent")  # runs on import!

# FIX — connect lazily inside a function or class
_conn = None

def get_connection():
    global _conn
    if _conn is None:
        _conn = psycopg2.connect("postgresql://localhost/agent")
    return _conn

# WRONG — breaks relative imports
# $ python tools/search.py
# ImportError: attempted relative import with no known parent package

# FIX — run as a module from the project root
# $ python -m my_agent.tools.search
# or add an if __name__ == "__main__" guard and use absolute imports inside it

in a subdirectory turns it into a namespace package, which is usually not intended. Namespace packages do not run initialization code and can cause subtle import resolution issues when the project root is on
Install the project as an editable package
Use absolute imports in application code
— they survive file moves and are unambiguous; use relative imports only inside tightly-coupled intra-package references
; IDE navigation and coverage reports both benefit
— prevents accidental exposure of internal helpers and documents the public surface explicitly
— never connect to databases, load ML models, or read large files at module level; use lazy initialization functions
Python's module system is designed for correctness, not speed. The import of a module runs its body exactly once per interpreter process (subsequent imports return the cached object from
). This caching means import overhead is paid at startup, not per request — once all modules are loaded, calling imported functions is as fast as calling local functions.

For agent services that handle many concurrent requests (via asyncio or threading), the shared module cache is thread-safe: Python guarantees that a module is executed only once even if two threads import it simultaneously (the import lock serializes this). The practical implication is that module-level singletons — like a shared
or an in-memory tool registry — are naturally shared across all coroutines and threads.

Import time becomes a scalability concern in serverless and edge environments where cold starts matter. Each import statement has a small overhead (file I/O, bytecode compilation on first run, execution). Strategies to minimize cold start time:
— move rarely-used imports inside the functions that need them; the module loads only when first called
— importing numpy, torch, or large transformer libraries at module level adds hundreds of milliseconds to startup; prefer conditional imports guarded by
; ensure the cache directory is preserved between deployments in containerized environments
— each installed package adds potential import overhead; audit dependencies with
— editable install; changes to source files take effect immediately without reinstalling
— a fast Rust-based resolver/installer that replaces pip + virtualenv for new projects; drop-in compatible with
— manages dependencies and virtual environments together; generates a
— useful when tools require native libraries (numpy, torch, FAISS) that pip cannot reliably build

# pyproject.toml — modern standard (PEP 517/518/621)
[project]
name = "my-agent"
version = "0.1.0"
requires-python = ">=3.11"
dependencies = ["anthropic>=0.25", "httpx>=0.27"]

[project.optional-dependencies]
dev = ["pytest>=8", "pytest-asyncio", "ruff", "mypy"]

[project.scripts]
agent = "my_agent.__main__:main"   # creates a CLI command

[tool.ruff]
line-length = 100

[tool.mypy]
strict = true
packages = ["my_agent"]

# Run the package as a module (sets sys.path correctly)
python -m my_agent "What is the capital of France?"

# Run a specific submodule
python -m my_agent.tools.search "openai function calling"

# Run all tests
pytest tests/ -v

# Type-check the package
mypy my_agent/

# Lint
ruff check my_agent/

No namespace isolation; name collisions as files grow
Circular imports possible; requires pip install -e .
No init code; subtler import resolution
Slightly more setup; ensures tests run against installed package
For most agentic AI projects, a regular package with the standard layout (package directory at repo root,

alongside it) is the correct choice. The src layout adds value when you need to ensure tests run against the installed wheel rather than the raw source tree — a concern for published libraries more than internal agent services.
in order. If a directory under attacker control appears early in
(for example, the current working directory, which Python adds by default when running scripts), an attacker can place a malicious file named
in that directory and intercept imports. Mitigations:
— isolate the project's Python interpreter from system packages and from the current directory
Never add untrusted directories to sys.path
— especially not user-controlled or world-writable paths
— they are resolved relative to the package root, not the script's directory, reducing the attack surface
is a potential supply-chain attack vector. A compromised dependency can execute arbitrary code at import time. Practical hardening:
rejects any package whose file hash does not match
— each added package expands the attack surface; prefer stdlib when the functionality is modest
Reliability: preventing import-time failures in production
A module that crashes at import time brings down the entire process. Prevent this by:
Deferring all I/O and network calls
to function scope, never module scope
so that a missing optional dependency degrades gracefully rather than crashing the service
as a mandatory CI step that catches missing dependencies before deployment
A module is a single .py file; a package is a directory with __init__.py. Both become importable objects whose names are attributes.

Structure agent code into four sub-packages: config, clients (LLM wrapper), tools (action implementations), and agents (orchestration). This produces a strict one-way dependency DAG with no circular imports.
Install the project with pip install -e . and a pyproject.toml so that imports work identically in the agent loop, the API server, and the test suite — no sys.path hacks.
Keep __init__.py files minimal: re-export public names and declare __all__. Business logic belongs in the module files, not in package init files.

Avoid side effects at module level. Database connections, model loads, and network calls must be deferred to function scope so that importing a module does not trigger expensive or failing operations.
Break circular imports by extracting shared code into a third module, or by using lazy (inside-function) imports as a last resort.
Always run modules with python -m my_agent, not python my_agent/__main__.py. The -m flag sets sys.path correctly so relative imports inside the package resolve.
In production, pin dependencies with a lockfile and verify hashes; audit for vulnerabilities before deploying; and smoke-test all imports in CI to catch missing packages early.
